event: English East India Company captain William Keeling becomes the first known European to sight the uninhabited islands, which later bear his name.
The islands begin appearing on navigational charts following further European sightings in the Indian Ocean.
British hydrographer Robert Tyler surveys and records detailed observations of the atolls.
British trader Captain John Clunies-Ross visits the islands and resolves to establish a settlement there.
Englishman Alexander Hare establishes the first settlement, bringing a harem and a group of Malay workers to the islands.
John Clunies-Ross arrives with his family and workers, beginning the Clunies-Ross dynasty's long control over the islands.
Alexander Hare departs after rivalry with Clunies-Ross, leaving the Clunies-Ross family in effective control of the population.
Charles Darwin visits aboard HMS Beagle and studies the atoll, contributing to his influential theory of coral reef and atoll formation.
Britain formally annexes the Cocos (Keeling) Islands, placing them under the British Crown.
Administrative responsibility for the islands is transferred to the Governor of Ceylon.
Queen Victoria grants the islands in perpetuity to the Clunies-Ross family, while control passes to the Straits Settlements.
A submarine telegraph cable station is established on Direction Island, making the atoll a key link in global communications.
The islands are incorporated into the Settlement of Singapore within the Straits Settlements.
In the Battle of Cocos, the Australian cruiser HMAS Sydney destroys the German raider SMS Emden, which had attacked the cable station.
A garrison is stationed on the islands during the Second World War to protect the strategic cable and wireless links.
Soldiers of the Ceylon Garrison Artillery mutiny on Horsburgh Island, one of the few mutinies in the British Empire during the war.
An airfield and military base are constructed on West Island to support Allied operations in the Indian Ocean.
Allied bombers based on the islands carry out raids in the closing campaigns of the Pacific and Southeast Asian theatres.
Administration of the islands passes to Singapore following the dissolution of the Straits Settlements.
Sovereignty over the islands is transferred from the United Kingdom to Australia, making them an Australian external territory.
A United Nations mission visits and reports on the living conditions of the Cocos Malay community under Clunies-Ross rule.
The Australian government compulsorily purchases most of the Clunies-Ross family's land holdings, ending their feudal dominance.
The Cocos Islands Council is established, giving the local community a formal voice in governance.
In a UN-supervised Act of Self-Determination, residents vote to integrate fully with Australia.
Western Australian state laws are extended to the territory, aligning local services and legal systems with the mainland.
The Pulu Keeling National Park is established on North Keeling Island to protect its unique seabird colonies and marine life.
Australia uses the islands as part of its offshore processing and surveillance amid increased asylum-seeker boat arrivals.
The Indian Ocean tsunami passes the atolls, prompting heightened attention to the territory's disaster preparedness.
The Australian government announces plans to upgrade the West Island airfield for expanded defence and surveillance use.
Australia advances a major redevelopment of the runway at the Cocos Islands airport to support maritime patrol aircraft.
